original_hypothesis.title: Per-Address Public Mint Limit Bypass via _safeMint Reentrancy Callback
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In NextGenCore.mint(), the per-address mint counter (tokensMintedPerAddress) is incremented AFTER _mintProcessing → _safeMint completes. _safeMint calls onERC721Received on the _mintTo address. A malicious contract at _mintTo can reenter MinterContract.mint() during this callback. The reentrant call reads tokensMintedPerAddress which hasn't been incremented yet, so the per-address limit check passes again. This allows minting arbitrarily many tokens (up to supply cap) from a single address, bypassing maxCollectionPurchases set in setCollectionData.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The state update ordering in NextGenCore.mint places the mint counter increment after an external callback via _safeMint. This is a classic reentrancy pattern where state reads in the guard precede state writes, allowing repeated bypass of the per-address limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _mintProcessing (which calls _safeMint with ERC721 callback) executes before tokensMintedPerAddress increment on line 202-204; reentrant call sees stale counter

preconditions: Collection is in public minting phase
Attacker deploys a contract that calls MinterContract.mint in onERC721Received callback
Attacker contract has sufficient ETH for multiple mint payments

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: tokensMintedPerAddress[col][_mintingAddress] += 1 occurs AFTER _safeMint callback on line 202-204 of NextGenCore.mint
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: onERC721Received callback → reenter MinterContract.mint → per-address check reads 0 → mint again → recursive
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker acquires N * maxAllowance tokens (where N = recursion depth) from a limited collection; secondary market value of excess tokens = profit

economic_questions: How many recursion levels are possible within block gas limit?
Does the same vulnerability apply to allowlist phase (tokensMintedAllowlistAddress)?
Can the attacker monopolize an entire limited-supply collection?
validation_requirements: Deploy reentrancy contract and attempt public mint exceeding maxCollectionPurchases
Verify tokensMintedPerAddress is not incremented during callback
Test recursion depth vs gas limit

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Secondary market value of excess tokens (could be 10-100x mint cost for desirable collections)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on collection demand; potentially thousands of %

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During public sale of high-demand collection
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Monopolizes limited supply, reducing availability for honest minters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other prospective minters of the collection
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Unable to mint due to supply exhaustion

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: maxCollectionPurchases value, collection supply, mint price
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm tokensMintedPerAddress read returns stale value during callback

validation_result.mechanism: ERC721 _safeMint reentrancy bypasses per-address mint limit
validation_result.impact_summary: A malicious contract receiving tokens via _safeMint can reenter MinterContract.mint() in the onERC721Received callback, bypassing the per-address public mint limit (maxCollectionPurchases) because tokensMintedPerAddress is incremented after the external call.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability exists in the interaction between MinterContract.mint() and NextGenCore.mint(). In NextGenCore.mint(), the function calls _mintProcessing() which invokes _safeMint(), triggering an onERC721Received callback on the recipient. The per-address counter (tokensMintedPerAddress) is only incremented AFTER _mintProcessing() returns. During the callback, a malicious recipient contract can reenter MinterContract.mint(). In MinterContract.mint(), the public phase check reads gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, msg.sender), which returns the stale (un-incremented) value. If the attacker contract is both msg.sender and _mintTo, each reentrant call sees a counter of 0, allowing unlimited recursive mints up to the collection supply cap or gas limit.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence from source code confirms the state update ordering defect. NextGenCore.mint() calls _mintProcessing() (which contains _safeMint with external callback) on the line before the tokensMintedPerAddress increment. MinterContract.mint() checks the counter before the minting loop and does not use any reentrancy guard.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: _mintProcessing (containing _safeMint with onERC721Received callback) executes before the tokensMintedPerAddress/tokensMintedAllowlistAddress increment. During the callback, the counter is stale, allowing reentrancy to bypass per-address limits.
_safeMint calls onERC721Received on _recipient if it is a contract, providing the reentrancy callback vector.

The per-address limit check (retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress) reads the stale counter during reentrancy. Each reentrant call sees 0 minted tokens for the attacker address, allowing the check to pass repeatedly.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker contract calls MinterContract.mint{value: price}(col, 1, 0, '', self, [], 0x0, 0) during public phase with _mintTo = self
2. MinterContract checks retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, attackerContract) → returns 0 → passes maxAllowance check
3. MinterContract calls gencore.mint(mintIndex, attackerContract, attackerContract, ..., col, 2)
8. MinterContract checks retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, attackerContract) → STILL returns 0 (not yet incremented)
9. The mint proceeds, triggering another _safeMint callback → recursive reentrancy
10. After recursion unwinds, tokensMintedPerAddress is incremented N times (once per gencore.mint return)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: MinterContract.mint() reads tokensMintedPerAddress (which is 0) → calls gencore.mint() → gencore.mint() calls _mintProcessing() → _safeMint() → onERC721Received callback → reenter MinterContract.mint() → reads tokensMintedPerAddress (STILL 0 because the increment in gencore.mint() at NextGenCore.sol lines 201-204 hasn't executed yet). The state update (tokensMintedPerAddress increment) happens AFTER _mintProcessing returns, violating the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script uses NextGenRandomizerNXT which calls setTokenHash synchronously within calculateTokenHash. This means the randomizer call in _mintProcessing completes before _safeMint, but this doesn't affect the vulnerability since the reentrancy vector is in _safeMint itself.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: (1) Deploy attacker contract with onERC721Received that reenters MinterContract.mint with sufficient ETH. (2) Wait for public minting phase. (3) Attacker contract calls MinterContract.mint with _mintTo = self, _numberOfTokens = 1. (4) During each _safeMint callback, reenter MinterContract.mint. (5) Each reentrant call passes the per-address limit check because tokensMintedPerAddress[col][attackerContract] is 0 during the entire recursion depth. (6) The supply check (collectionTokenMintIndex <= viewTokensIndexMax) works correctly because circulationSupply IS incremented before _safeMint, so each reentrant call computes a valid new mintIndex. (7) Each reentrant call requires msg.value >= getPrice(col), so the attacker needs to fund the contract with price * N ETH. (8) Gas limit bounds the recursion depth but multiple transactions can be used.
validation_result.impact_assessment: An attacker can mint far more tokens than maxCollectionPurchases allows per address in a single transaction, potentially monopolizing a limited collection's supply. This directly violates the per-address mint limit invariant that protects fair distribution. The same vulnerability applies to the allowlist phase via tokensMintedAllowlistAddress, though exploiting that also requires a valid merkle proof. The financial impact equals the secondary market value of excess tokens acquired beyond the intended limit.
recommended_mitigations: Move the tokensMintedPerAddress and tokensMintedAllowlistAddress increments BEFORE the _mintProcessing call in NextGenCore.mint() (follow Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern)
Add a reentrancy guard (e.g., OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard) to MinterContract.mint() and/or NextGenCore.mint()
Consider using _mint() instead of _safeMint() if the onERC721Received callback is not needed, eliminating the callback vector entirely
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all NextGen contracts using fixturesDeployment.js. 2. Create and configure a collection with maxCollectionPurchases = 1, totalSupply = 10, public phase active. 3. Deploy ReentrancyAttacker contract implementing onERC721Received that calls MinterContract.mint with _mintTo = self, sending required ETH. 4. Fund attacker contract with 10x mint price. 5. Call attacker.attack() which calls MinterContract.mint once. 6. Verify attacker contract holds more than 1 token (the maxCollectionPurchases limit).
uncertainties: Gas limit bounds the recursion depth - exact depth depends on the randomizer used (NXT is synchronous and cheaper, VRF/RNG are async but calculateTokenHash still costs gas)
With the NXT randomizer, calculateTokenHash calls setTokenHash synchronously which uses storage writes (~20k gas each), limiting recursion depth further
additional_testing_needed: Measure maximum recursion depth under 30M gas block limit with NXT randomizer
Test the same attack on the allowlist phase with tokensMintedAllowlistAddress
Test whether airDropTokens in NextGenCore has the same reentrancy issue (it uses _safeMint via _mintProcessing before incrementing tokensAirdropPerAddress)
